bullets: Shipped 10 projects (React, TS, GraphQL, Sass) and 5 projects (Twig, JS, jQuery, Less) with shared components and different themes.
Mentored 4 junior engineers who later progressed to mid/senior roles.
Set up a Turborepo monorepo with 10 packages and 4 Next.js apps.
Built and maintained a 60+ component UI library adopted by 4 internal teams.
Implemented i18n with namespaces across apps.
Added ARIA roles, focus management and keyboard navigation across core flows.
Configured Docker, ran Azure DevOps releases.
Built trading dashboards for Citi with real-time AG Grid.
Built complex AG Grid views with saved layouts, tabs, and resizable panes using flexlayout-react.
Raised test coverage above 50% with Jest.
Shipped a production admin project in 13 months: 38 pages, 8 tables, 16 create/edit pages.
Implemented MSAL React auth and React Hook Form.
Wrote unit tests with Jest on core modules.
Delivered features and UX fixes in a Next.js product during a 5-month contract.
Collaborated with design and backend to close high-impact UX issues.

text.line1: I'm a Senior Frontend Engineer building user-friendly Next.js and React applications. I enjoy turning complex tasks into simple, elegant, and user-centered solutions.
text.line3: Day-to-day I work with React, TypeScript and Next.js, with a focus on design systems, performance and accessibility. I'm always learning and enjoy sharing what I know with the team.
text.line5: Outside work I'm on the PlayStation, in the kitchen with a slab of meat, or with my family.

text.line0: Learning English is a project that started during the React marathon by Zara Zakharova.
text.line1: During the marathon, we got acquainted with React, its lifecycle, and learned to work with forms and Firebase.
text.line10: The word appears in the database (or in local storage for unauthorized users) and is displayed below the input field.
text.line11: When you click on the word, the card flips over, showing the translation on the back.
text.line12: The word can be marked as memorized or deleted, removing it from your word bank.
text.line2: We mastered working with props and states, as well as passing data between components.
text.line3: At the beginning, we wrote our own Redux to understand its principles, and later integrated it.
text.line4: We used icons and forms from the AntDesign library for the interface.
text.line5: We connected the dictionary API and Firebase storage to save users' words.
text.line6: In the new version of the project, which I rewrote in Next.js, modern technologies and new features have been added.
text.line7: Now, Google is used for authentication, and the data of unauthorized users is stored locally in localStorage.
text.line8: Instead of Firebase storage for saving words, a custom database is used that stores cards for all users.
text.line9: To use the site, you need to enter a new English word in the input field.

ganttChart.text: This is the testing task. Here I use the technologies that I use in the previous project.

fourText.line0: Subsequently, I completely rewrote the project based on modern Next.js.
fourText.line1: I uploaded data for all Pokémon, created my own database, and formatted it according to my needs using Prisma for convenient data management.

secondText.line0: This project is entirely written using hooks, which significantly simplified the transition to a new React team where I quickly adapted by converting class components to functional ones.
secondText.line1: I was also able to share this knowledge with the team, which contributed to improving our collaborative practices.

text.line0: Pokédex is the result of my advanced React marathon under the guidance of Zara Zakharova.
text.line1: During the marathon, I gained great experience working with new technologies and design, which included a detailed style guide with colors, gradients, fonts, and their sizes.
text.line3: In the initial version of the project, I implemented the following technologies:

thirdText.line0: Unfortunately, the API I was working with used the HTTP protocol and is currently unavailable.
thirdText.line1: However, I enhanced the project by adding filters for types and attacks, and also implemented modal windows using "createPortal".

text.line0: I find it important to have a professional portfolio site that displays all of my projects and accomplishments.
text.line10: At this point, I thought it would be good to add a back button. I found an interesting canvas animation and applied it to the back button.
text.line12: The user can go to the projects page (all the best projects with the description of applied technologies used and links to Github. I think live video demos will live here too).
text.line14: The user can go to the 'contacts' page to contact me. On the page of my reports, the skills that I share with others can be shown.
text.line16: At the beginning of writing the code, I didn't know what stack my portfolio site would have. Looking back in the time I really wanted to write it in React, but my basic knowledge hadn't been enough for it, so I had used ES6. Since the time I have gained much experience in React, and now I think I can perform much better. I realized that I needed to customize and add functions in a way that would demonstrate my programming skills, so I wrote it in native JS.
text.line2: At first I had no idea how the site of a portfolio should look and what should be in it. I started enquiring and reading articles on this topic.
text.line20: Subsequently, I created a monorepo (turborepo) for my portfolio, rewrote it using Next.js with modern technologies, and moved almost all my pet projects into this monorepo. The main goal of this portfolio website is to showcase the projects I've created, and the site itself is also considered a project!
text.line4: I drew up a plan, decided on a color scheme, sketched a couple of ideas and set to work. The thing I understood about it was it had to be unique and creative. Fuh, it was difficult!
text.line6: First, the user is taken to the home page where I used the fluid effect. I did not find such a library, but I found places where this effect was applied and borrowed it from there, slightly transforming it in my own way. Here on the main page, you can interact with the animation by pressing SPACE. Flashes of fluid animations will appear, by pressing "P". If you want to stop the animation, you can press pause. By clicking and swiping across the screen with a mouse or finger, the animation is brought into action.
text.line8: Then the user can go to the 'About me' page. On this page I introduce my personal story, the stack of technologies I own, work experience and other facts about me =)

text.line0: Here I managed to write an online game in which several people can play at once.
text.line1: This is cool experience with sockets and server side.
text.line3: Stack: HTML / SASS / JS / Webpack5 / Websocket (socket.io) / EsLint / Prettier.
text.line4: To check the functionality of the game and chat, you can launch the game in several tabs.

snake.description: A classic Snake game with zero dependencies. Uses the DOM for rendering the grid and requestAnimationFrame as the main game loop for consistent updates. The code is structured with classes; core entities (Game, Cell) encapsulate their logic, interacting via composition. Keyboard input is handled through a queue to prevent stuck keys and invalid turns.
snake.title: Native HTML, CSS, JS (no frameworks).

solitaire.description:
All cards are PNGs rendered with Canvas.
The architecture uses classes with a “Composition over Inheritance” approach: cards, decks, and game states encapsulate their own behavior while interacting through composition rather than rigid inheritance. This allows for flexible feature expansion and easy updates. Instead of heavy inheritance chains, everything is split into small, independent classes. The main idea is to keep adding new features simple.
solitaire.li3: Clean, minimal code without unnecessary dependencies.
solitaire.title: Native HTML, CSS, JS (no frameworks).

text.line0: While working in EvoPlay, I received a task to reproduce the Thanos effect.
text.line2: The main purpose was to make the disintegration of the pictures like in the movie Avengers 3.
text.line3: The pictures should disintegrate into ash and fly in the wind.
text.line4: After surfing on the Internet, I found many examples of this implementation.
text.line5: Since I needed to split only pictures into atoms but not the content, I found these examples unsuitable for this purpose.
text.line6: However, ordinary canvas is much better for it, so I made this effect without using the html2canvas library.

2048.title: Plain HTML, CSS, JS (no frameworks).
2048.description: Class-based 2048 with zero dependencies. The grid renders via the DOM and CSS Grid. Default board is 5×5; size and styling are controlled via cols/rows and CSS custom properties. Each move runs as a pipeline: slide → merge → spawn, synchronized by transitionend/animationend. New tiles spawn as 2 or 4 (25% for 4). Win appears once a tile exceeds 2048; game over when no moves remain.
2048.li1: Class engine: Game, Board, Cell, Box, EventListener; composition over deep inheritance.
2048.li2: DOM + CSS Grid with CSS custom properties.
2048.li3: Keyboard and touch; waits for animations to finish.
2048.li4: Slide/merge algorithm grouping by rows and columns.
